title: The big data era arrives
content: In the 2000s, data growth accelerated across many industries. A common model described this with three Vs: volume (how much), velocity (how fast it arrives), and variety (how many forms it takes). Together, these pressures exposed limits in traditional tools.
next: If one machine can't keep up, what can?

title: Many hands make light work
content: The breakthrough was distributed computing: instead of one powerful, expensive machine, use many cheap, ordinary ones working together as a team. Split the data and the work across them, and the group can handle far more than any single computer ever could.
next: Who first showed this could work at massive scale?

title: Google shows the way
content: Google was already operating at web scale. In 2003 and 2004 it published influential papers describing distributed storage and parallel processing with MapReduce. These designs helped shape the architecture of early big-data systems.
next: How did everyone else get to use these ideas?

title: Hadoop opens the door
content: In 2006, the open-source Hadoop ecosystem translated these ideas into widely available software. It combined distributed storage (HDFS) with distributed processing (MapReduce). This made large-scale data processing accessible to many organizations using clusters of commodity machines.
next: But where do you put all that raw data?

title: The data lake idea
content: Instead of forcing data into a rigid warehouse, why not pour all of it — raw, in any format — into one big, cheap repository? That's a data lake. You store first and decide on structure later, when you actually read the data.

title: Cheap, almost limitless storage
content: Cloud object storage — like Amazon S3, launched in 2006 — made the data lake practical. It was cheap, effectively limitless, and pay-as-you-go. Crucially, it separated storage from compute, so you could grow each independently.
next: Why was 'structure later' such a big deal?

title: Schema-on-read flips the rules
content: Warehouses demanded structure before loading. Data lakes flipped this to schema-on-read: dump the data in now, and apply structure only when you read it. This flexibility was a gift for data scientists and for new, messy data types.
next: So was big data the happy ending?

title: MapReduce was painful
content: MapReduce had important limitations. It often wrote intermediate results to disk between steps, which increased latency for iterative workloads. It also required relatively low-level programming patterns, which made many tasks slower to develop and maintain.
next: And what about the lake itself — did it stay clean?

title: Lakes turned into swamps
content: Data lakes had no built-in rules or reliability. Anything could be dumped in, with no guarantees about quality or consistency. Many lakes decayed into 'data swamps': vast, messy, and so untrustworthy that nobody wanted to use them.
next: So what did companies gain, and what did they lose?

title: A trade-off, not a triumph
content: Data lakes offered lower-cost storage and high flexibility, but early lake patterns often lacked strong governance and reliability controls. Many companies ended up managing two imperfect worlds: structured but rigid warehouses, and flexible but harder-to-govern lakes.
next: What the era really needed was a faster, friendlier engine.

title: A better engine is coming
content: The missing piece was a processing engine that was fast, flexible, and far easier to use than MapReduce. In a research lab at UC Berkeley, exactly such a project was taking shape. It would change big data forever.
